text:
Admission Date:  1981-11-11              Discharge Date:   1986-09-27

Date of Birth:  2011-03-28             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Levaquin

Attending:Sean
Chief Complaint:
Hypotension; loss of consciousness; diarrhea

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Central venous line placement.

History of Present Illness:
This is a 79-year-old woman with ESRD on HD, HTN, and
hyperlipidemia who is admitted from rehab facility after episode
of weakness, slurred speech, and hypotension during physical
therapy.  She recently had a UTI and c.diff infection as noted
below.
.
She was recently admitted to Pam Specialty Hospital Of Corpus Christi North in early 12 of this year
for musculoskeletal chest pain.  At that time she was found to
have a UTI and was treated with a course of antibiotics.  She
subsequently developed c.diff (as per patient and daughter
report) and was treated at rehab.  Patient states that diarrhea
resolved, but then returned over the past 4 days.  She says that
during PT this morning, she developed lightheadedness and
dizziness; she felt hypotensive as she had on prior occasions.
Patient denies acutal syncope, but per nursing home report she
actually lost consciousness.  Ms. Sanford was brought to Pam Specialty Hospital Of Corpus Christi North
where code stroke was called.  Patient was evaluated by
neurology team who found no deficits.  A CT scan of her head was
negative for any acute pathology.
.
Patient was found to be hypotensive on admission to 82/60.  She
was given 2L of IVF without much effect.  White count was 8.1
and lactate was 2.2.  ECHO performed at the beside was negative
for large effusion.  Patient received IV flagyl (was ordered for
vanc and ceftriaxone but never got them), had a RIJ placed, and
was started on levophed.  On transfer from ED, vitals were: 97.3
77, 94/45, 17, 96% on 2L.

Past Medical History:
-CHF
-ESRD on HD (since 08)- Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday
-hypertension
-hyperlipidemia
-gout
-osteoporosis
-dry senile macular degeneration
-rheumatic fever (last occurrence at age 12)
-anemia
-s/p open fracture of intertrochanteric section of femur 10-14
(slipping on wet steps; she denies any loss of balance or
presyncope)
-several left sided rib fractures 10-14
-h/o c.diff (2014-04-21) with recurrence->severe c.diff this
admission

Social History:
She has a 45-year history of smoking 1 PPD. No current tobacco.
She drinks alcohol rarely (4-5 times per year), and coffee
rarely.
.
Retired elementary school secretary (32 years).  Lives in
[**State 12000**] with her divorced son; her other son also lives in
Richard Harvey.  She spends about half the year in [**State 108**].  She
occasionally visits daughter in Bradley Jimenez.  She is currently in a
rehab facility in 90649 Morris Court Apt. 958
West Lisa, TN 66280 (was moved from one in [**State 108**]
to be closer to family) after a recent hip fracture and repair
in 1989-09-30.  She will live with her daughter once leaving
rehab.

Family History:
Mother had breast cancer, died of colon cancer at 88.  Father
died of lung cancer at 64.  Paternal grandmother had heart
disease, stroke, breast cancer, and thyroid disease (had
surgery), died at 76 from stroke after fall.  Maternal
grandfather died of MI at 94 or 95.  Uncle had breast cancer,
died of lung cancer at 94.  Cousin had thyroid disorder.

Physical Exam:

VS: Afebrile, HR: 77, B: 99/40 (on levophed), SP02 95% on RA
GENERAL:  Well appearing, no acute distress
NECK:  JVP elevated 3cm above clavicular line
CHEST:  Crackles in left lower base
CARDIAC:  Regular rate and rhythm

Loose BM x2 yesterday; x1 this shift
Exam at 0710
Gen: NAD
CV: RRR, slight systolic murmur
Pulm: CTAB, no crackles/wheezes
Abdomen: soft, NT, +BS
Ext: WWP, no erythema, no edema.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. Sanford is a 79-year-old woman with end stage renal disease
on hemodialysis, HTN, and hyperlipidemia who developed a recent
UTI and c.diff infection while at a rehabilitation facility
status post fall.  She was admitted to the intensive care unit
with hypotension.  A bedside echo showed no large effusions.  A
stroke code was called, but a Neurology evaluation was negative
for deficits and head CT was negative for acute intracranial
process.  She clinically improved and was transferred to the
floor on hospital day 2.  Below is a summary of her hospital
course by problem:
.
1. Hypotension: she was hypotensive to the 80s/40s on admission
and was resuscitated with fluids and was briefly on vasopressors
in the intensive care unit but quickly weaned off.  Her
hypertension medications were held.  Her blood pressure improved
with crystalloids, she remained afebrile, her white count
remained normal, and her hypotension was attributed to volume
loss from diarrhea and possibly the toxic effects from
clostridium difficile.  She clinically improved overall on
antibiotics for c.diff, was otherwise without symptoms or signs
of sepsis physiology afterwards, and remained hemodynamically
stable for the remainder of her admission.
.
2. Clostridium difficile and diarrhea: She was first initiated
on intravenous flagyl and vancomycin and then transitioned to
oral vancomycin alone on hospital day 2 when she was noted to
have another c.diff infection.  Her diarrhea clinically
improved, and the decision was made to continue a 14-day course
of oral vancomycin.    She was initially on a bland diet but
tolerated a regular diet by hospital day 3 without difficulty,
once her diarrhea improved.
.
3. EKG changes: On admission, she was noted to have ST
depressions in leads V4, V5, and V6, and her BNP was elevated to
greater than 24,000.  The changes were attributed to demand
ischemia secondary to her hypotension.
.
4. Hypertension: At home she is on amlodipine, clonidine, and
labetalol.  These were held due to her hypotension on admission.
 As her blood pressure improved, her home dose of labetalol was
added on hospital day 3.  On recommendation from Renal,
amlodipine and clonidine were not restarted, and she was
instructed to follow up with her primary physician and dialysis
team to restart these medications in the next few days to goal
blood pressure <130/80.
.
5. End stage renal disease:  The Renal team continued to follow
her course and make recommendations.  She was restarted on her
home dialysis schedule.
.
She remained on prophylactic subcutaneous heparin and an H2
blocker.  She was discharged on hospital day 5 in good
condition, with instructions to complete a total 14 day course
of vancomycin.  She will return back to her rehabilitation
facility and follow up with her primary physician.

Medications on Admission:
1. amlodipine 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.
2. Aranesp (polysorbate) Injection
3. Colace 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO once a day.
4. famotidine 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q48H (every 48
hours).
5. folic acid 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

6. labetalol 200 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a
day).
7. polyethylene glycol 3350 17 gram/dose Powder Sig: One (1) PO
BID (2 times a day) as needed for constipation.
8. sevelamer HCl 400 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO TID
W/MEALS (3 TIMES A DAY WITH MEALS).
9. temazepam 15 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO HS (at
bedtime) as needed for insomnia.
10. nitroglycerin 0.4 mg Tablet, Sublingual Sig: One (1)
Sublingual once a day as needed for chest pain: may repeat x 3,
if you need more than once please call your physician.
11. clonidine 0.1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO every eight
(8) hours as needed.
12. Dulcolax 5 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One (1)
Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO once a day.

Discharge Medications:
1. folic acid 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

2. famotidine 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q48H (every 48
hours).
3. vancomycin 125 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO Q6H (every
6 hours) for 11 days: Please be sure to take all your doses of
antibiotics and complete the entire course of medication.
Disp:*qs Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*
4. acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO Q6H (every
6 hours) as needed for pain or fever.
5. B complex-vitamin C-folic acid 1 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Cap
PO DAILY (Daily).
6. labetalol 200 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a
day).
7. Aranesp (polysorbate)  Injection
8. peg 3350-electrolytes 227.1-21.5-6.36 gram Powder in Packet
Sig: One (1)  PO twice a day as needed for constipation.
9. sevelamer HCl 400 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO three
times a day: Take with meals.
10. temazepam 15 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO at bedtime
as needed for insomnia.
11. nitroglycerin 0.4 mg Tablet, Sublingual Sig: One (1)
Sublingual once a day as needed for chest pain: 11 repeat up to
three times.  If you need it more than once, please call your
physician.

Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Grays Harbor Community Hospital LivingCenter - Heathwood - 5231 Jillian Freeway
East Stacey, SD 75245-5261

Discharge Diagnosis:
Clostridium difficile infection.
Anemia.


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker
or cane).


Discharge Instructions:
Ms. Sanford, it was a pleasure taking care of you.  You were
admitted to Nye Regional Medical Center after
developing diarrhea and losing consciousness at your
rehabilitation center.  You were found to have very low blood
pressure and were given intravenous fluids and medications to
help raise your blood pressure in the emergency department.  You
were also given antibiotics for your diarrhea.  You were seen by
general neurology and the stroke service and they both believed
that there was no indication that you had a stroke.

Since your blood pressure remained low, you were transferred to
the ICU, where you continued to receive fluids and antibiotics.
Your diarrhea improved with oral vancomycin, and tests confirmed
that you have a clostridium difficile infection causing your
diarrhea.  You will continue oral antibiotics until 2011-02-19 for
this infection.

You were also started back on hemodialysis and will continue
your usual schedule.  Your blood pressure increased and you were
restarted on your home dose of labetalol.  After discussion with
Renal specialists, it was decided not to restart your amlodipine
or clonidine at this time.  Your blood pressures are somewhat
elevated now, and you will need to adjust these medications with
your primary physician.

You are being discharged back to your rehabilitation facility.

We started you on oral vancomycin 125 mg every 6 hours (four
times a day).  Please continue to take this until 03-21.

We also started you on neprhocaps (B complex-vitamin C-folic
acid), 1 tablet daily, as recommended by the kidney doctors.

We stopped your amlodipine and clonidine.  You can restart them
as needed, but should be careful to ensure that your blood
pressure does not drop too low.

We stopped your colace and dulcolax.  You may restart them, if
needed, after your diarrhea resolves.

Followup Instructions:
She should resume her normal Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday
dialysis schedule at 90649 Morris Court Apt. 958
West Lisa, TN 66280.

The patient needs to follow-up with her PCP after discharge from
rehab.
